How do you differentiate between the natural vegetation and the planted vegetation? The same variety may be... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: TYPES OF FORESTS, NATURAL VEGETATION, 43 NATURAL VEGETATION, 44 INDIA : PHYSICAL ENVIRONMENT. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Natural Vegetation is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that you might have surely gone to a park if you live in a city or to a mango, guava or coconut orchard, if you live in a village.

The text explains that how do you differentiate between the natural vegetation and the planted vegetation?

The text explains that the same variety may be found growing wild in the forest under natural conditions and the same tree may be the planted one in your garden under human supervision.

The text explains that natural vegetation refers to a plant community that has been left undisturbed over a long time, so as to allow its individual species to adjust themselves to climate and soil conditions as fully as possible.

The text explains that india is a land of great variety of natural vegetation.

The text explains that himalayan heights are marked with temperate vegetation; the Western Ghats and the Andaman Nicobar Islands have tropical rain forests, the deltaic regions have tropical forests and mangroves; the desert and semi desert areas of Rajasthan are known for cactii, a wide variety of bushes and thorny vegetation.

The text explains that depending upon the variations in the climate and the soil, the vegetation of India changes from one region to another.

The text explains that on the basis of certain common features such as predominant vegetation type and climatic regions, Indian forests can be divided into the following groups: TYPES OF FORESTS (i) Tropical Evergreen and Semi Evergreen forests (ii) Tropical Deciduous forests (iii) Tropical Thorn forests (iv) Montane forests (v) Littoral and Swamp forests.

The text explains that tropical Evergreen and Semi Evergreen Forests These forests are found in the western slope of the Western Ghats, hills of the northeastern region and the Andaman and Nicobar Islands.

The text explains that they are found in warm and humid areas with an annual precipitation of over 200 cm and mean annual temperature above 22oC.

The text explains that tropical evergreen forests are well stratified, with layers closer to the ground and are covered with shrubs and creepers, with short structured trees followed by tall variety of trees.

The text explains that in these forests, trees reach great heights up to 60 m or above.

The text explains that there is no definite time for trees to shed their leaves, flowering and fruition.

The text explains that as such these forests appear green all the year round.

The text explains that species found in these forests include rosewood, mahogony, aini, ebony, etc.

The text explains that the semi evergreen forests are found in the less rainy parts of these regions.

The text explains that montane Forests In mountainous areas, the decrease in temperature with increasing altitude leads to a corresponding change in natural vegetation.

The text explains that these provide numerous direct and indirect advantages to our economy and society.

The text explains that accordingly, the Government of India proposed to have a nation-wide forest conservation policy, and adopted a forest policy in 1952, which was further modified in 1988.

The text explains that according to the new forest policy, the Government will emphasise sustainable forest management in order to conserve and expand forest reserve on the one hand, and to meet the needs of local people on the other.

The text explains that the forest policy aimed at : (i) bringing 33 per cent of the geographical areas under forest cover; (ii) maintaining environmental stability and to restore forests where ecological balance was disturbed; (iii) conserving the natural heritage of the country, its biological diversity and genetic pool; (iv) checks soil erosion, extension of the desert lands and reduction of floods and droughts; (v) increasing the forest cover through social forestry and afforestation on degraded land; (vi) increasing the productivity of forests to make timber, fuel, fodder and food available to rural population dependant on forests, and encourage the substitution of wood; (vii) creating of a massive peoples movement involving women to encourage planting of trees, stop felling of trees and thus, reduce pressure on the existing forest.

The text explains that the importance of forests in tribal economy is well-known as they are the source of sustenance and livelihood for tribal communities.

The text explains that farm Forestry Farm forestry is a term applied to the process under which farmers grow trees for commercial and non-commercial purposes on their farm lands.

The text explains that it is estimated that about 4-5 per cent of all known plant and animal species on the earth are found in India.

The text explains that the main reason for this remarkable diversity of life forms is the great diversity of the ecosystem which this country has preserved and supported through the ages.

The text explains that some of the important reasons of the declining of wildlife are as follows: (i) Industrial and technological advancement brought about a rapid increase in the exploitation of forest resources.

The text explains that (iii) Pressure on forests mounted due to lopping for fodder and fuelwood and removal of small timber by the local people.

The text explains that what steps have been initiated by the government in this direction?

The text explains that for the purpose of effective conservation of flora and fauna, special steps have been initiated by the Government of India in collaboration with UNESCO’s ‘Man and Biosphere Programme’.

The text explains that source : Annual Report 2024-25, Ministry of Environment and Forests, Government of India.

The text explains that choose the right answer from the four alternatives given below.

The text explains that (a) Bihar (c) Uttarakhand (b) Uttar Pradesh (d) Odisha and conservation of Himalayan Musk deer have also been launched by the Government of India.

The text explains that bIOSPHERE RESERVES A Biosphere Reserve is a unique and representative ecosystem of terrestrial and coastal areas which are internationally recognised within the framework of UNESCO’s Man and Biosphere (MAB) Programme.

The text explains that (a) One (c) Twelve (b) T w o (d) Four (v) Which one of the following proportion of area of the country was targeted to be under forest in Forest Policy of India?

The text explains that under what climatic conditions are tropical evergreen forests develop?

The text explains that (ii) What do you understand by social forestry?

The text explains that (iv) What is the difference between forest area and forest cover?

The text explains that (i) What steps have been taken up to conserve forests?

The text explains that (ii) How can people’s participation be effective in conserving forests and wildlife?

The text explains that on the outline map of India, mark and label the following.

The text explains that (ii) Biosphere reserves of Nanda Devi, Sunderbans, Gulf of Mannar and Nilgiri.

The text explains that (iii) Mark the location of Forest Survey of India Head Quarter.

The text explains that list the trees, bush and shrub species found around your school.

Source: NCERT Class 11 India Physical Environment, Chapter 5: Natural Vegetation
